text: The surgeon performs a diagnostic hysteroscopy, visually inspecting the interior of the uterus, which reveals a polypoidal endometrium without major abnormalities. The procedure transitions to a laparoscopic myomectomy.

Following the introduction of laparoscopic ports, a broad ligament fibroid is visible, deeply impacted into the pelvis. The fibroid causes significant bulging and stretching of the adnexa on one side.
The surgeon observes adhesions involving the sigmoid colon, which will need to be carefully divided before proceeding with the myomectomy.
The surgeon begins retroperitoneal dissection to address the large fibroid, which is visibly impacting the pelvic region.

The surgeon identifies the ureter under the posterior peritoneal fold and proceeds with uterine artery ligation using a posterior approach, avoiding the anterior approach due to the visibility of the ureter.

The surgeon makes an incision over the ureter, deepening it to expose the great vessels. The ureter is visible with peristalsis over the medial leaf of the broad ligament. Dissection is performed parallel and lateral to the ureter to locate the uterine artery.
Following further dissection, a vertical chord-like structure is revealed, identified as the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon identifies the obliterated hypogastric artery and its medial branch, which is seen coursing vertically upwards parallel to the obliterated hypogastric artery. The uterine artery is visible in this orientation.
The surgeon dissects between the hypogastric and internal iliac arteries, pulling the internal iliac to expose the medial branch, identified as the uterine artery.
Due to space constraints, the surgeon places a ligature around the internal iliac artery instead of the uterine artery, planning to remove it later by using a shoelace knot technique.

The surgeon forms a shoelace knot using two threads, with the loop created on the left hand. The right hand goes under the threads to make a single throw, grasping the short end of the thread and securing the knot completely.
The surgeon places a shoelace knot over the internal iliac artery stem, tightening it comfortably despite space restrictions in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and carefully pushes it away from the surgical field. The internal iliac artery is secured with a ligature, and the ureter is visible running medial to the artery and ligature.

The surgeon completes the procedure on the left side, securing the internal iliac artery with a shoelace knot. The decision is made to proceed with the myomectomy without performing internal iliac ligation on the right side.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum and uses a myoma screw to hitch and gradually pull up the fibroid, deepening the incision as the fibroid is elevated.

The surgeon uses a bipolar vessel sealer and cutter to rapidly cut tissue with minimal bleeding, demonstrating efficient single-handed operation without the need for additional instruments like scissors or cautery.

The surgeon cuts open the capsule of the fibroid and inserts a myoma spiral into the fibroid, applying traction. The medial leaf of the broad ligament is pushed down, moving the terminal part of the ureter along with it.

The surgeon observes uretic peristalsis near the base of the fibroid, visible due to prior dissection. Attempts to cauterize the base of the fibroid reveal increasing venous ooze. The fibroid is noted to have a large, broad-based, sessile attachment to the lateral border of the uterus.

The surgeon exposes the right uterine artery using a posterior approach, with the ureter clearly visible, and prepares to ligate the artery to prevent potential torrential bleeding after further cuts.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and performs dissection parallel and lateral to it, exposing the internal iliac artery, also known as the obliterated hypogastric artery. The dissection continues medially to trace the origin of the uterine artery.

The surgeon separates the areolar tissue to reveal the uterine artery, which is identified as the first medial branch of the internal iliac artery. The uterine artery is prepared for ligation, with visible space restrictions due to the assistant's hand retracting nearby.

The surgeon observes unexpected bleeding from beneath the uterine artery while passing a suture, suspecting injury to the superficial uterine vein. The decision is made to ligate or cauterize the uterine artery using bipolar energy.

The surgeon uses a left-handed grasper to retract the ureter while the right-handed grasper coagulates the uterine artery to stop blood flow. Both uterine arteries are now coagulated, allowing for the safe separation of the myoma without risk of torrential bleeding. The space is confirmed, and vasopressin is injected using a visual vasopressor injection needle.
The surgeon injects vasopressin into the uterus and the fibroid to achieve maximum devascularization, visibly blanching both surfaces before making an incision.

The surgeon uses a visual vasopressor injection needle to puncture the uterine substance, obtaining a clear aspirate indicating it is safe to inject vasopressin. Upon obtaining a blood-stained aspirate, the site is flushed, and a new injection site is chosen. Aspiration confirms the absence of blood, allowing the remainder of the vasopressin solution to be safely injected.
The surgeon incises the fibroid, making a cut slightly above its base to preserve part of the pseudo capsule. This follows the injection of vasopressin and bilateral ligation of the uterine arteries.

The surgeon makes an incision around the circumference of the fibroid, leaving space between the fibroid and the uterus to manage potential bleeding. The fibroid is visibly separating from the uterine wall as the incision progresses.

The surgeon uses a bipolar vessel sealer and cutter to excise the last part of the fibroid. The base of the fibroid is now visible, revealing that it originates from the lateral wall of the cervix rather than the broad ligament as initially thought.
The surgeon begins suturing the defect left after fibroid removal using regular vicryl sutures, taking deep bites to ensure closure despite the absence of barbed sutures.

The surgeon uses a figure of eight suture technique to close the uterine defect, taking extra tissue from the superior surface for added strength. Two figure of eight sutures are placed to ensure complete closure of the defect.

The surgeon tightens the knots of the second figure-of-eight suture placed on the uterine defect, ensuring hemostasis. The sutures create a secure pedicle with minimal bleeding observed from the base. The suture is then cut, and hemostasis is confirmed visually.

The surgeon checks for hemostasis, revealing clear anatomy of the area. The great vessel is visible, along with the vicryl suture forming a shoelace knot over the left internal iliac artery. The next step involves releasing the ligature to confirm there is no bleeding from the base of the sutured fibroid.

The surgeon carefully removes the shoelace knot ligature from the pedicle, gently pulling the identified thread to release the knot. The removal is smooth, showing no resistance or damage to the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon pulls out the vicryl suture, revealing no trauma to the internal iliac artery, which shows good pulsations. The peristalsis of the ureter is visible medial to the artery. The uterine artery ligature is removed, and hemostasis is checked, showing slight oozing from the sutured base of the pedicle. The lateral pelvic wall on the left side is clearly visible.

The surgeon identifies the ureter, which is clearly visible and demarcated, ensuring it is not in the path of the bleeding area. The surgeon proceeds to cauterize the bleeding site using bipolar cautery, confident that the ureter is safely out of harm's way.
The surgeon applies bipolar cautery to the base of the pedicle, successfully reducing the bleeding without causing damage to the ureter, which is safely positioned away from the cauterized area.

The surgeon performs irrigation to confirm hemostasis, revealing clear visualization of the external iliac vessels on the left side, the anteriorly running ureter, and the white cord-like internal iliac artery. The internal iliac artery is seen medial to the ureter, ensuring no damage has occurred to the ureter.
The surgeon places an adhesion barrier membrane over the sutured area to prevent postoperative adhesions, marking the completion of the myomectomy procedure.
